speaker: interviewer
text: So you were telling-- saying about how really out of your way you were going to make yourself liked by the mother of your girlfriend. And she just wouldn't accept it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it just-- it just-- I think I was probably hurt as much as I was confused.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you want to marry her daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. We had a very close relationship. And we were very compatible. And at that point in my life, I think the ambiguity probably wasn't as clear in my mind as it is now. Even though I was-- she was not Jewish, I was being rejected by a mother because I was Jewish. And I still wanted to marry her.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I don't know what direction I was going. I wasn't very Jewish. I was non-observant. Although I would never-- well, let me put it this way. I didn't walk around with a star on my forehead. I didn't announce it. But if somebody asked me what religion was I, obviously, I was Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the service. I had Hebrew on my dog tags. So it was obviously I'd made a point about it. And I was able to-- met other Jewish people in service. And we did socialize. But I wasn't gravitating towards Jewish organizations and such.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you participate in anything Jewish in the Navy?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Navy? No. I think, though, a few times, wherever I was, and I think maybe in Hawaii-- I was in Hawaii for a couple of years-- I think they had High Holy Day services at the local chapel. And they had a rabbi come by there. And I did go. And it was foreign to me, on one hand, because I have no training in-- but I felt home anyway, because I knew I was part of my own people. You know, for however, it was once or twice a year, you know, it happened. And that was about the extent of it. So-- so that was a little bit of, you know-- I mean-- it was just--

speaker: interviewer
text: It sounds heartbreaking.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was. So we finally, we realized, because she was-- it was tough on-- on the lady, too. Because she didn't want to-- she was still her mother. And she obviously didn't want to-- she realized, or maybe she was threatened by her mother, that if you marry me, then it would alienate her. And they've come through, they've gone through a lot too. Even though they were born in America, it was just very, just days after the war. But it was her mother who suffered mostly. And they felt very sorry for her. There was no father, they supported her and all that. So it was decided that it wasn't to be. So we did separate. And then, I did continue on, though, in San Diego. I went to a junior college. And I went to college, and graduated college, and went on to graduate school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work while you were in college?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yeah, I worked. I had all kinds of odd jobs. Nothing, you know, nothing of any consequences. Nothing to-- just-- just enough to earn money to support myself through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you eligible for GI rights?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had GI rights. At that time, it was like $175 a month. You know, it really wasn't enough. It was enough to pay for my rent. But I still had to-- I still had to work to support myself. But I had no problems. I was always managed to find enough work, and to support myself. And it was a state university. And it certainly wasn't out of my reach. And at that point, I did-- I started to associated with the Jewish, to some extent, the local Jewish community. Not very strong, but just as a social-- more of a social level. I didn't belong to any temples. But more of a social level, I started to gravitate to some-- some peripheral Jewish community events, and some singles and all that. It was never a large part of my life. But I was beginning to become a little bit out of my shell at that point. But I still had problems talking about things. Because, at that point, when somebody found out what my background was, I would get this, oh, I feel so sorry for you type of attitude. And I just detested it. I just didn't know how to handle that. So I would just, basically, just avoid it. And then if somebody else found out about, I said I'll only tell you if you want it for historical purposes. Or if you really want to know for your own information. But the minute you start feeling sorry for me, you know, I just don't know how to handle it. So it wasn't until many years later that-- actually, it wasn't until I started to, I was married into this family in 1980, that I really started coming out of this particular shell.

speaker: interviewer
text: About the war experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dealing with the war experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly. Yeah, in fact for many years I lied about where I was born. I mean people say, well, where are you from. I said, I'm from New York. Oh. Because I was able to get by with a New York accent. And then, every now and then, somebody with some kind of an ear for languages would say, well, you know, that's New York, but there's also something else there. You know, well, you know, I lived in Germany for a few years, and just mixed of that. And I would sort of downplay it a little bit. You know, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were in graduate school, and then you completed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, then I finished graduate school. And then I went to work for a large corporation. And I went to work for them on my strength of speaking German. I actually learned German, I re-learned German in graduate school. It was called the International School for-- Graduate School for International Management in Phoenix. And it was a two year graduate school that strictly trained you in international management and business. You had to have a bachelor's degree to get into that school in the first place. And so I went there, chose European studies, and my foreign language was French. And after the first two weeks I said, dummy, go back to German. You'll have a wonderful time rolling it off your tongue.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went in and I just aced my German classes, re-learned German, got the pronunciation right and all that, picked up the European studies program, did very well, you know like, 1%, 2% of my class. Got picked up with a very good job offer from American Can Company. Went to New York, trained me there, oriented me in New York, and then they moved me back to Europe. Now, when you were in New York, did you contact your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was married at the time. I had my first marriage at that time. I had gotten married while I was in my junior year in college. And we contacted, we began to have somewhat of a-- somewhat of a normal relationship. And I was there-- I knew I was going to be going to Europe. I mean, they had already told me-- actually, I'd made some trips there anyway, to get me started. And, today, I probably wouldn't do it. But then, I liked to travel. And I wanted to travel first class for a change, or at least business class, rather than--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hitching a flat car.

speaker: interviewee
text: --steerage. So they moved me to Germany, of all places. And they settled me in Hamburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you share with them at all what your background was?

speaker: interviewee
text: To some extent, yes. They knew I was born in Germany. My company, they looked at my background record. They knew of my work experience, which was my academic experience was good. They knew that I spoke the language. They tested me for my understanding of getting along in a European environment. They knew that I had traveled extensively. But they didn't know it was steerage. But they knew I-- knew I-- I can talk to kings and paupers type of a thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: But not about really your refugee experience.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. I don't remember if they even asked. Or if I put it down, they didn't pursue it. They just figured it was enough that I knew, they could drop me into any European city and that I would not get lost. So that's what it was. And so I must have been in my early 30s at the time. They moved me to Hamburg on my own. I became their marketing, their manufacturer's representative for their Dutch packaging plant. And I was responsible for all of Germany. And so I was basically on the road in Germany, selling and servicing the packaging products in their different factories and clients. And so it was a good experience. It was a wonderful experience. That experience, my experience of them, they didn't have to tell-- my company didn't have to tell me what to say, what not to say. I mean, they obviously had enough confidence in me to pay me a good salary to move over there. But, on my own, I walked into a-- if I walked into a factory of nothing but Germans in Bremerhaven or Stuttgart someplace, obviously, my first words out of my mouth are not that I'm Jewish, I was in the camps. I would speak German with them, but it was beginning, was with an American accent. So I'd say, well, yeah, I'm from America. But my parents are German. And I learned German very well. But I'm still with-- they accepted it. It was fine. They wanted to speak English with me anyway, because this was in 1969, 1970. It wasn't as prevalent as it is today. It was-- it was a treat for an American to go over to Europe and learn, to work as an expatriate, get paid very good money. You were living very well. And you didn't see too many American businessman traveling around at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you walk into a factory with nothing but Germans, the only Americans they saw were tourists, not a businessman working over there. So we'd go to lunch. And I wanted to practice my English with you. And most of the time, my German was better than their English then. Today, they speak better English. But it wasn't in those days. So between my German and their English, we communicated very well. I was able to sell my equipment there, and service it, and troubleshoot, and so forth. So, occasionally, I would get friendly with one or two of the people I would call on a lot. And we would, it would be on more than just business. We would socialize a little bit, especially if I would be overnight in one of the cities.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they'd say, well, you're staying in a hotel. Come over to my house for dinner. Because they wanted to be more than business. I'm from America, and all this. Occasionally, I would tell them, by the way, I was born here. I would begin to-- it was interesting reactions I would get from some of the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you feel a need to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I became a little brazen. I wanted to test them a little bit. Maybe I was beginning to think I was living a lie. And I felt comfortable, at that point, that I wasn't going to jeopardize a relationship. I felt comfortable. They were younger. They were a little bit younger. They were not the old timers. They were probably more in their 30s and 40s, and I felt that they were a little bit more liberal. They wouldn't be quite as-- they wouldn't be still stuck in the old German Hitler Aryan mentality, which I'll explain in a minute, where I experienced in Holland.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was able to explain. And they were-- some of the time they were very gracious about it. Of course, they never knew. I didn't know. You know, I'd heard about it. You know, type of-- no German will ever admit to you that they knew what was going on, ever.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was able to talk to Germans. Sometimes, we would socialize during lunch. And I would say, without me telling them I was born there, I was still an American with a German, I'd say, curious to know, you know, I have some people that I know in America who came from Europe who were here during the camps and all that. They were like neighbors. And they were telling me all this. And so I'm curious to know what you know. You know, I would sort of twist it around. Yeah, it was a shame. But nobody ever knew. Never. I never met a person. Now I didn't meet thousands of people, but I met a lot of them. The conversation would come around to it. I was getting a little more aggressive. But I would always cloak myself behind being an American. I never found one person that ever said, well, yeah, I knew about, or, yeah, down with those Jews. They were very patronizing and nice about it. So I was really never able to get underneath anybody's skin. But I traveled a lot to Holland, because the factory was in Rotterdam. And the first few times I traveled to Rotterdam on business, I knew they all spoke German. So you know, I would communicate with the shopkeepers in German. And I would get the cold shoulder. And then, I began to realize that if you're like, if you're over 50 or 60, they hate the Germans. They always hated the Germans. Because the Germans just devastated Holland. But then, I say well, do you speak English. Oh, yes, we speak English. I'm from America. Oh, they loved the Americans. Loved the Americans.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I learned, at that time, of what language to speak to what age group. And because I was beginning to speak a little Dutch, picking up Dutch and a little Danish, too. I travelled in that area. So there was actually a level there of what to speak to. So it was kind of an experience. I see. Very clever. Well, and then-- and then-- you stayed with the American Can Company for a while.

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed with them until 1972. And they realized that the European venture was a bust. It was just-- that's just another story entirely. They just couldn't compete against the European packaging companies. American Can Company was big here, but they just couldn't handle the European market. The Europeans were much more advanced in packaging equipment, in plastics, and in color technology than American Can Company was. American Can Company was making tin cans for Dole pineapple, and that was their big thing, and beer cans. They got into this paper ball packaging years later, and they were really catching up to many more sophisticated companies in Sweden and in England and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did that affect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they finally said that we're shutting down the international division. You're all coming back. When I say you all, me, and they had another fellow in Sweden, and another one in Holland. We went sort of like a team over there. And they brought us back. And they offered me a job. Actually, the fellow in Holland decided to leave the company and stay in Holland. And the fellow in Sweden decided to stay in Holland. And he stayed there. I came back to America. And I didn't like the job offers they had, so I came to-- came back to California in 1972. And just picked up a new life over here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were single at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was still married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Still married.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're still married. And came over here, and got separated from my wife in 1973, '74. Stayed single for a number of years. We met Joan here, began our life together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And along the way, you've had children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I've had one child, from my first marriage. And that was-- he was born in 1970. And he was born right-- the year after I graduated from graduate school. Just before-- he was born in Connecticut, just before we moved the family over to Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, with Joan, you have a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Joan has three children. And when Joan and I got together, they were-- they were all 10 or under. And they're all in their late 20s, early 30s now. So I'm effectively part of their family. I have been all these years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said that around 1980 is when you came out of your shell about the war experience. How did that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was Joan. Joan is a wonderful Philadelphia Jewish broad, if I may say that. She's just a wonderful Jewish person. She's steeped in Judaism. And I'm very lucky that she took a lot of interest in me. More than a husband and a lover, she wanted to bring this thing out. And she started to encourage me to talk more about it. And she actually got me in touch with Sarah Moskowitz, who wrote a book in starting to interview some of the child survivors. And through some communication that Joan had with somebody else, she-- I was one of the first interview groups. And it was like a rap session of children who had gone through the war, either as survivors in the camps or as hidden children. And this happened, I guess, in 1981, '82, or so forth. I don't remember exactly. And so, I went in and it was sort of like we had some discussions, groups, rap sessions, they called them. A half a dozen of us. And, you know, little by little, I started talking more about it. And I felt a greater level of comfort by talking to other people who were in the camps. And I realized that maybe I was feeling sorry for myself. I realized some of them went through a lot worse than I did. I've realize that children who were hidden had their own problems, maybe a lot worse than I did, because they didn't know from day to day where they were going, living in a basement, or in an attic or something. At least I knew where I was.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was able to expand by my experiences a little bit more with other people, and seeing that they went through other experiences. So I can incorporate mine. And I didn't feel-- I never really felt myself-- I always felt myself lucky.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the thing is that Theresienstadt was not a death camp. And a lot of people, Jews also, will say, well, it wasn't one of the bad camps. And I agree with them. I'm not going to sit there and say, I mean, there are people who went through horrors. And I know that. Those who survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I've recognized that Theresienstadt was a lucky place to be. And I'm extremely lucky, because I found out many years later that about 50,000 children went through that camp. And then about 100 of them made it at the end of the war.  And I was one of those hundred. It was a very eerie feeling, when you know that you're part of a small group. So I was very, very lucky. But still, on the other hand, I'm saying, well, was it a normal life. Did it do me any good? I don't know. It's hard to say. You know, I think I feel I'm well-adjusted, except that, you know, I suppose I could be better adjusted if I'd grown up in more of a Jewish home, if it wasn't a different environment. I don't know. I did want to ask you one question about being Jewish. It seems like it's been such a burden, and a source of, really, torture for you, in so many points in your life. Why be Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why be Jewish? It's a wonderful question. Well, first of all, I have no choice. I mean, I truly do not have a choice. I mean, I'm Jewish because the world-- the world says I'm Jewish. I mean, I went through it-- I went through it because I was Jewish. It was not my choice. On the other hand, the more I learn about it, the more I read about it, and the more people that I meet, I find there's a lot of pride in it. Sometimes, I'm a little bit uncomfortable with an overabundance of Jewish pride. I don't, I feel, sometimes, personally, I think it's a little bit overbearing when I hear people say, well, look at this brilliant scholar. I mean, he's Jewish, of course. Deep down inside, I feel great. I feel wonderful. Because I feel I'm a part of that. I feel great because our values are wonderful, because of education. Even, whatever little Jewish, Judaism my mother had, she recognized that I had to have an education. Even living in a dinky apartment in Berlin, she knew that I had to read, I had to do something, because she recognized that whatever little education I had was going to help me on later in life. And I had no values when I left. I mean, whatever values I had when I left the service, I mean, I could have gotten any kind of job. But you know, it was uppermost to get an education. And where that came from, I don't know. But I know now that, in Judaism, education is foremost. It's very important. It was just never an issue. And I went through it without any problems, even though I had nobody telling me you've got to get it, you've got to get it. It's just that, well, maybe, I don't know. I hate to say that it's inherent, but somehow or other, it came through to me. And yeah, there is pride. And if I could learn to be more aggressive and more outgoing about it, I would be maybe just as overbearing as Joan is, as some other people. But I'm still very conserve-- reticent about it, even though, inside, I feel it. But I'm not-- there is a lot of pride. And when I do go to-- when I do go to services, it's just really great to be with everybody else who, you know, we're like of one mind. And although I'm not observant in the traditional sense, I will never, ever deny the fact I'm Jewish. And people at work, I work in a traditional non-Jewish environment, in a bank, I don't think there are five people in my division of 180 that are Jewish. But everybody knows I'm Jewish. They know I take a personal day during the High Holy Days. They know why I take it. I have no problem with it. And when they see I have no problem with it, they accept me, I think. I don't make any excuses for it. You know, so, in my own way, I think I'm-- I don't know if I'll ever go any further of it. I don't know Hebrew. Maybe when I go to Israel, which we've put off two trips already because of other pressing things. Maybe once I hit Israel and come back, maybe there'll be this spark that will force me to go beyond that. But I know a lot of our friends go to Israel all the time. But they don't know any more Hebrew than I do. I don't know if they're any more Jewish than I am. It's just a wonderful experience for them. And they have that over me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe it'll just another step and a connection.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In forging a connection.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you very much for speaking with us this morning. I understand we have some pictures to look at.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So why don't we go and set those up? Is there anything that I haven't asked you about though, before we shut off, that you'd like to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gosh, no, I don't know. It's hard to-- no. I thank you for the opportunity to allow me to share, you know, my little life with millions of others who don't have the opportunity to share it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we're honored that you invited us into your home to hear.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your story is very different, because you were so much younger when it started. [PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS] Why now? If you can say.

speaker: interviewee
text: For all those people who can't tell their story. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is a picture of me, I believe taken in the apartment in Berlin, probably, I must've been-- looks like I may be four or five years old. Probably a year or two before we were taken to Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably 1940 or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is my mother, and sister, and myself, probably in the displaced DP camp in Deggendorf. My sister must be a year or so old there. It's probably just prior to us arriving, coming to America. And that was probably in 1946. Well, probably closer to 1947. We came over here in August, so that was probably taken in that year. That is my sister sitting on my mother's lap and me in the DP camp in Deggendorf. Probably taken in 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister's name is Evelyn. And, of course, my mother, Hilde Berlowitz. That was taken in the Navy boot camp in San Diego in 1958. I'm at the-- on the right. And those are two of my buddies. I do not recall their names at that time. That is-- I'm holding my son, Erik Daniels. He appeared to be a few months old, taken in Connecticut in 19-- probably in the middle of 1970. That is with my son Erik, who appears to be about six years old at the time, taken in 1976 in a little park in West Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: California?

speaker: interviewee
text: California. I'm giving my wife Joan a wonderful hug because I love her so much. And this was probably taken in our home, probably in 1980 or '81.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Los--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, our home in Los Angeles, California. This is the wedding picture of my daughter and son-in-law, taken at the Sinai Temple in Los Angeles, California, on February 28, 1993. Starting from your right is Don, my son, my mother-in-law, Ruth, my wife, Joan, Michael Hirsch, my son-in-law. Of course, Ilana. Next to Ilana, on the left, is myself, is me. Rahm, and my son, Erik.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Rahm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rahm is also one of-- my stepson.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my son, Erik, who you saw on the extreme left on that last wedding picture, with his wife, Julie. This was taken in Portland, Oregon, July 1, 1994. It was his wedding picture. To the right is my son-in-law, Mike Hirsch, and my daughter, Ilana. In between Erik and Ilana, you see Rahm. And then, on the left of Julie is Dahn, and my wife Joan and me.
